title: AeroBridge-TTA: Test-Time Adaptive Language-Conditioned Control for UAVs
abstract: 语言引导的无人机（UAV）常因执行不匹配而失败，而非推理或感知错误：当真实动力学与训练状态存在差异（质量变化、阻力偏移、执行器延迟、风力干扰）时，规划轨迹与控制器跟踪能力之间出现脱节。为此，我们提出AeroBridge-TTA——一种面向语言引导的控制流水线，通过测试时自适应（TTA）专门解决此类失配问题。该架构包含三个模块：映射指令为子目标的语言编码器、基于子目标与学习隐变量的自适应策略，以及根据观测状态转移在线更新隐变量的TTA模块。在相同领域随机化条件下，针对13种失配场景的5项语言引导无人机任务测试中，AeroBridge-TTA在分布内任务中与强基线PPO-MLP模型性能持平，并在全部5项分布外（OOD）任务中胜出，平均提升22.0个百分点（62.7% vs. 40.7%）；整体8.5个百分点的增益完全来自OOD场景。通过仅改变步长α的同权重消融实验证实，隐变量更新本身带来了4.6倍的OOD性能提升。
html_content:
AeroBridge-TTA: Test-Time Adaptive Language-Conditioned Control for UAVs
Lingxue Lyu*
University of Pennsylvania
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States
lingxuelyu@alumni.upenn.edu
Abstract
Language-guided unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) often fail not from bad reasoning or perception, but from
execution mismatch
: the gap between a planned trajectory and the controller’s ability to track it when the real dynamics differ from training (mass changes, drag shifts, actuator delay, wind). We propose
AeroBridge-TTA
, a language-conditioned control pipeline that targets this gap with test-time adaptation. It has three parts: a language encoder that maps the command into a subgoal, an adaptive policy conditioned on the subgoal and a learned latent, and a test-time adaptation (TTA) module that updates the latent online from observed transitions. On five language-conditioned UAV tasks under 13 mismatch conditions with the same domain randomization, AeroBridge-TTA ties a strong PPO-MLP baseline in-distribution and wins all 5 out-of-distribution (OOD) conditions,
+
22.0
+22.0
pts on average (
62.7
%
62.7\%
vs.
40.7
%
40.7\%
); the
+
8.5
+8.5
pt overall gain comes entirely from the OOD regime. A same-weights ablation that only changes the step size
α
\alpha
shows the latent update itself is responsible for a
4.6
×
4.6\times
OOD lift. Code, checkpoints, and rollout videos:
https://github.com/lyulingxue/AeroBridge-TTA
.
Keywords—
Unmanned aerial vehicles, language-conditioned control, test-time adaptation, domain randomization, reinforcement learning, sim-to-real.
Figure 1
:
AeroBridge-TTA closes the execution-mismatch gap on unseen dynamics.
On the same language-conditioned navigation task, the PPO-MLP baseline fails under a composite out-of-distribution perturbation (gray, dashed,
×
\times
). The same AeroBridge-TTA checkpoint reaches the goal under nominal, mass
+
40
%
+40\%
(OOD), strong wind, and combined-OOD, by adapting a latent from observed transitions online.
I
Introduction
Large language models (LLMs) make it tempting to command robots in plain English instead of writing waypoints by hand
[
1
,
2
,
3
]
. For unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) the appeal is even stronger: an operator can just say “fly low to the target while avoiding the obstacle”
[
4
,
5
]
.
The reasoning and perception layers of these systems have improved a lot
[
6
,
7
]
, but one bottleneck is often ignored:
execution mismatch
. Even when the language module gives the right high-level plan, the low-level controller can fail when the real dynamics differ from training. Payload changes, damaged propellers, actuator delay, and wind all push the realised trajectory away from the intended one. The drone does not misunderstand the command; it just cannot fly the plan.
Online adaptation has a long history in other robot domains, and the lesson is consistent. In humanoid robotics, recovering balance from a sudden push needs millisecond-scale strategy switching. One line of work uses hand contact to stabilise a falling humanoid via real-time optimal control
[
8
]
, and later realises the same strategy in closed loop on a physical robot
[
9
]
. More general planners pick the right contact mode from the available environment affordances by searching a contact transition tree
[
10
]
, instead of committing to one fixed strategy. Legged locomotion uses a rapid motor adaptation module to handle new body parameters and terrain at deployment
[
11
]
. In agile flight, learned residual models adapt to wind in-flight
[
12
]
or compensate ground effect during landing
[
13
]
. Even a simple platform like a self-balancing bike needs the controller to react to the
real
dynamics, not the assumed ones
[
14
]
; classical adaptive control formalises this idea with Lyapunov update laws
[
15
]
. The same message shows up in autonomous driving: small mismatches between a calibrated and the actual longitudinal model compound quickly and need online correction
[
16
]
. The common conclusion is that robust execution needs a closed-loop adaptation step
inside
the controller, not just a better open-loop plan.
We propose
AeroBridge-TTA
for the language-conditioned UAV setting. Our contributions are:
1.
Execution-mismatch hypothesis.
We name execution mismatch as a primary failure mode of language-conditioned UAV control, separate from perception or reasoning errors, and we show that domain randomisation alone does not close it (Sec.
III
).
2.
Structured language-to-control pipeline.
A three-stage architecture, language encoder
→
\rightarrow
subgoal
→
\rightarrow
adaptive policy, that separates semantic understanding from motor execution. Each module can be improved on its own; the language frontend in particular is drop-in and can be replaced by a stronger LLM/VLA without retraining the controller (Sec.
IV
).
3.
Test-time adaptation for UAV control.
A small TTA module that updates a latent
𝐳
t
\mathbf{z}_{t}
from each observed transition, so the policy compensates dynamics mismatch
without retraining
. The TTA head is around 6.5K parameters and runs as one feed-forward pass per step, suitable for 50 Hz real-time control (Sec.
3
).
We test AeroBridge-TTA on five language-conditioned tasks and 13 mismatch conditions, and compare it to a classical PID controller and to a strong PPO-MLP baseline trained with the same domain randomisation.
II
Related Work
Language-conditioned robot control.
SayCan
[
3
]
grounds instructions in affordances; RT-2
[
1
]
and PaLM-E
[
2
]
train vision-language-action models end-to-end; LM-Nav
[
17
]
reuses pre-trained models for outdoor navigation. Aerial work covers vision-and-language navigation
[
4
]
and LLM-based task planning
[
6
,
5
]
. Most of these works assume the low-level controller is good enough, which is exactly what breaks under dynamics mismatch.
UAV control and learning.
Classical work uses geometric controllers
[
18
]
and trajectory optimisation
[
19
]
. RL has been applied to quadrotors
[
20
]
and to champion-level drone racing
[
21
,
22
]
. Neural-Fly
[
12
]
and Neural Lander
[
13
]
learn residual dynamics, but they are not language-conditioned.
Sim-to-real and adaptation.
Domain randomisation
[
23
,
24
]
trains over a dynamics distribution. RMA
[
11
]
learns an adaptation module for legged robots. Test-time training
[
25
]
and self-supervised policy adaptation
[
26
]
update the model at deployment. Online calibration
[
16
]
corrects the dynamics for autonomous cars at test time. We bring this adaptation-at-execution idea to language-conditioned UAV control.
Robust execution in robotics.
The need for closed-loop adaptation is well known in humanoids, which must recover from disturbances in real time
[
8
,
9
,
10
]
, and in balance problems
[
14
]
. Our take is the same: robustness under mismatch needs an adaptation step
inside
the controller, not only a smarter plan.

VI
Discussion
DR and TTA are complementary.
Inside the DR range, both methods are saturated and basically tied. The gap opens on OOD, where AeroBridge-TTA wins every condition (up to
7.7
×
7.7\times
on Combined-OOD). A latent that summarises the recent transition residuals is the right tool for perturbations DR cannot enumerate, especially when several axes (mass, drag, delay, wind) interact at once. Pushing the DR range wider would help in principle, but the effective grid grows multiplicatively with the number of axes, while one TTA latent costs us a single small MLP.
Why the Combined-OOD gap is so large.
Stacking mass, drag, delay, and wind together creates a regime that the baseline never sees, even with wide DR. AeroBridge-TTA does not need to have seen this exact combination at training time: as long as the residual signature is in the span of what
f
ψ
f_{\psi}
learned, the latent moves in a direction the policy already knows how to react to. This is why we observe a
7.7
×
7.7\times
gain on Combined-OOD, while Wind-strong (a single, mild axis) gives a much smaller gain.
Limits.
Adaptation cannot beat physical limits: at Mass
+
50
%
+50\%
both methods fail because the rotors run out of thrust. A fixed
α
\alpha
is fine inside the actuator envelope; gating
α
\alpha
on residual magnitude is left for future work. The language frontend is drop-in (Eq.
5
); a stronger LLM or VLA can replace
ϕ
\phi
without retraining the controller. The TTA head is about 6.5K parameters and one forward pass per step, suitable for 50 Hz real-time control, unlike gradient-based TTA which needs a backward pass each update
[
25
]
. Everything in this paper is in simulation; real flights would also need sensor noise, communication delay, and actuator nonlinearity.
VII
Conclusion
AeroBridge-TTA targets execution mismatch in language-conditioned UAV control with test-time adaptation. A single language-conditioned model does 5 tasks at
97.6
%
97.6\%
average SR. Under 13 mismatch conditions with the same DR, it ties PPO-MLP in-distribution (
Δ
=
0
\Delta{=}0
) and wins every OOD condition (
+
22.0
+22.0
pts,
7.7
×
7.7\times
on Combined-OOD,
+
33.3
+33.3
on Mass
+
40
%
+40\%
). The same-weights ablation isolates the mechanism: raising
α
\alpha
from
0
to
0.1
0.1
lifts average OOD success from
16.8
%
16.8\%
to
77.5
%
77.5\%
. The execution-mismatch view and this TTA formulation should carry over to other language-conditioned robots that face dynamics they did not train on.

preview_text:
Language-guided unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) often fail not from bad reasoning or perception, but from execution
  mismatch: the gap between a planned trajectory and the controller's ability to track it when the real dynamics differ
  from training (mass changes, drag shifts, actuator delay, wind). We propose AeroBridge-TTA, a language-conditioned
  control pipeline that targets this gap with test-time adaptation. It has three parts: a language encoder that maps the
  command into a subgoal, an adaptive policy conditioned on the subgoal and a learned latent, and a test-time
  adaptation (TTA) module that updates the latent online from observed transitions. On five language-conditioned UAV
  tasks under 13 mismatch conditions with the same domain randomization, AeroBridge-TTA ties a strong PPO-MLP baseline
  in-distribution and wins all 5 out-of-distribution (OOD) conditions, +22.0 pts on average (62.7% vs. 40.7%); the +8.5
  pt overall gain comes entirely from the OOD regime. A same-weights ablation that only changes the step size $α$
  shows the latent update itself is responsible for a $4.6\times$ OOD lift.

AeroBridge-TTA: Test-Time Adaptive Language-Conditioned Control for UAVs
Lingxue Lyu*
University of Pennsylvania
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States
lingxuelyu@alumni.upenn.edu
Abstract
Language-guided unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) often fail not from bad reasoning or perception, but from
execution mismatch
: the gap between a planned trajectory and the controller’s ability to track it when the real dynamics differ from training (mass changes, drag shifts, actuator delay, wind). We propose
AeroBridge-TTA
, a language-conditioned control pipeline that targets this gap with test-time adaptation. It has three parts: a language encoder that maps the command into a subgoal, an adaptive policy conditioned on the subgoal and a learned latent, and a test-time adaptation (TTA) module that updates the latent online from observed transitions. On five language-
one_line_summary: 该论文提出一种用于无人机语言条件控制的测试时自适应方法，主要关注执行不匹配问题，与给定关键词中的强化学习、运动控制等有微弱关联，但核心方法不涉及VLA、扩散模型等。
